name: Eos.EosType
comment:
This module is only used internally by the library. It is not meant to be used
directly by the user, unless you're using the code generation tool to build
dynamic forms.

@docs EosType

unions.name: EosType
unions.comment:
This includes all of the native types, declared in [abi\_serializer::configure\_built\_in\_types()](https://github.com/EOSIO/eos/blob/de78b49b5765c88f4e005046d1489c3905985b94/libraries/chain/abi_serializer.cpp#L89-L127).

It only doesn't support `bytes`. If you need support for `bytes`, please open an
issue on GitHub

name: Eos.ExtendedAsset

aliases.name: ExtendedAsset
aliases.comment: An extended asset is simply an [Asset](Eos-Asset), but with a contract [Name](Eos-Name)
aliases.type: { amount : Basics.Float, symbol : Eos.Symbol.Symbol, contract : Eos.Name.Name }

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Decode an [ExtendedAsset](#ExtendedAsset) from JSON.

values.name: encode
values.comment: Encode an [ExtendedAsset](#ExtendedAsset) into JSON.

name: Eos.Name

unions.name: Error
unions.comment:
The `Error` type represents all of the possible errors when converting a [Name](#Name)
[fromString](#fromString).

    fromString "0123456789"
    --> Err (InvalidCharacters ( '0', [ '6', '7', '8', '9' ] ))

    fromString "eosio."
    --> Err DotInLastPlace

    fromString ""
    --> Err TooShort

    fromString "a.very.long.name"
    --> Err TooLong

unions.name: Name
unions.comment:
Names are strings of up to 12 characters in length. They can include
characters a-z, 1-5, and the dot character (except for the last character).

In order to generate a `Name`, you can use [fromString](#fromString) or
[decoder](#decoder).

To display a `Name`, use the [toString](#toString) function. You can also
[encode](#encode) a `Name`

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
Decode a [Name](#Name) from JSON. You can use this to receive a [Name](#Name)
from the blockchain or some server. It already does all of the validation necessary
to ensure the [Name](#Name) is valid.

values.name: encode
values.comment:
Encode a [Name](#Name) into JSON. You can use this to send a [Name](#Name) to
the blockchain or some server.

values.name: errorToString
values.comment:
You can use this function to convert an [Error](#Error) into a human-readable
`String`.

values.name: fromString
values.comment:
Convert a regular `String` into a [Name](#Name). Since there are some
restrictions on the possible names, this function can fail with an [Error](#Error).

    fromString "eosio.token"
        |> Result.map toString
    --> Ok "eosio.token"

    fromString "0123456789"
    --> Err (InvalidCharacters ( '0', [ '6', '7', '8', '9' ] ))

    fromString "eosio."
    --> Err DotInLastPlace

    fromString ""
    --> Err TooShort

    fromString "a.very.long.name"
    --> Err TooLong

values.name: toString
values.comment:
Convert a [Name](#Name) to a regular `String`. This is useful if you want to
display names in your application.

    fromString "eosio.token"
        |> Result.map toString
    --> Ok "eosio.token"

name: Eos.Permission

unions.name: Permission
unions.comment:
There are two permissions by default: Owner and Active. However, each account
may have arbitrary permissions (that's what `Custom` is for).

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Decode a JSON value into a Permission

values.name: encode
values.comment: Turn a permission into a JSON value.

values.name: toString
values.comment: Turn a Permission into a String

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Decode a PublicKey.

values.name: encode
values.comment: Encode a [PublicKey](#PublicKey) into JSON.

values.name: fromString
values.comment: Turn a String into a PublicKey

values.name: toString
values.comment: Turn a PublicKey into a String

name: Eos.Query

unions.name: Cursor
unions.comment:
Used to set the lower bound or upper bound with [withLowerBound](#withLowerBound)
or [withUpperBound](#withUpperBound). You can get one of these from [send](#send).

unions.name: Index
unions.comment: The Index to use when sending a [Query](#Query).

unions.name: Query
unions.comment:
The `Query` type. It's only exposed so that the CLI can have access to it.
You shouldn't construct it by hand.
cases: { scope : String.String, indexPosition : Maybe.Maybe Eos.Query.Index, lowerBound : Maybe.Maybe Eos.Query.Cursor, upperBound : Maybe.Maybe Eos.Query.Cursor, limit : Maybe.Maybe Basics.Int, reverse : Basics.Bool, baseUrl : String.String, contract : String.String, table : String.String, decoder : Json.Decode.Decoder response }

aliases.name: Response
aliases.comment:
A response from a [Query](#Query). It contains the results, and also
pagination data. You can check if there is more data to be fetched with the
`hasMore` field. If there is, you can use the `nextCursor` field to fetch the
next page of data with [withLowerBound](#withLowerBound) or
[withUpperBound](#withUpperBound).
aliases.type: { hasMore : Basics.Bool, nextCursor : Eos.Query.Cursor, result : List.List response }

values.name: map
values.comment:
Map the response of a Query. This is here so the CLI can use it. You probably
shouldn't need to use it.

values.name: send
values.comment:
Send a [Query](#Query) to EOSIO.

You shouldn't need to construct a [Query](#Query) by hand. Instead, use functions
generated by the CLI. They live in the Table.Query module.

Some parameters of the query are defined when running the CLI (like `baseUrl`).
Others can be modified with functions like [withLimit](#withLimit) and
[withBaseUrl](#withBaseUrl) (in case you need to override it).

This returns a [Response](#Response), since there may be pagination in the result.
If you want to paginate, keep the [Cursor](#Cursor) returned in the
[Response](#Response) and use [withLowerBound](#withLowerBound) or
[withUpperBound](#withUpperBound) in the next query.

    import Eos.Query
    import Http
    import Table
    import Table.Query

    type Msg
        = GotAccountData (Result Http.Error (Eos.Query.Response Table.Accounts))

    queryAccounts : Cmd Msg
    queryAccounts =
        Table.Query.accounts { scope = "henriquebuss" }
            |> Eos.Query.withLimit 20
            |> Eos.Query.send GotAccountData
values.type: (Result.Result Http.Error (Eos.Query.Response response) -> msg) -> Eos.Query.Query response -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg

values.name: withBaseUrl
values.comment:
Redefine the base url to [send](#send) a [Query](#Query). This is already
defined when running the CLI, so you shouldn't need this function often.

values.name: withLimit
values.comment: Set how many entries to fetch on [send](#send).

values.name: withLowerBound
values.comment:
Define the lower bound of a [Query](#Query). This is used to paginate the
results. You can get a [Cursor](#Cursor) from a [Response](#Response), which you
can get with [send](#send).

values.name: withReverse
values.comment: Define if the [Query](#Query) results should be reversed.

values.name: withUpperBound
values.comment:
Define the upper bound of a [Query](#Query). This is used to paginate the
results. You can get a [Cursor](#Cursor) from a [Response](#Response), which you
can get with [send](#send).

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Decode a Signature.

values.name: encode
values.comment: Encode a [Signature](#Signature) into JSON.

values.name: fromString
values.comment: Turn a String into a Signature

values.name: toString
values.comment: Turn a Signature into a String

name: Eos.Symbol

unions.name: Error
unions.comment:
When creating a [Symbol](#Symbol), there are two things we validate: the
precision and the symbol code. This type represents that.

unions.name: PrecisionError
unions.comment:
Precisions must be >= 0. This type represents the error that can happen when
trying to create a [Symbol](#Symbol) with a negative precision.

unions.name: Symbol
unions.comment:
A Symbol is what identifies a currency, and is made of a `precision` and a
[SymbolCode](Eos-SymbolCode). For example, the symbol `1,EOS` is a symbol with
precision 1, and a symbol code of `EOS`.

The precision number determines how many decimal digits can be used. In the
example above, someone can have 0.1 EOS, but not 0.01 EOS.

values.name: code
values.comment:
Get the [SymbolCode](Eos-SymbolCode) of a [Symbol](#Symbol)

    import Eos.SymbolCode

    fromPrecisionAndCodeString 1 "EOS"
        |> Result.map (code >> Eos.SymbolCode.toString)
    --> Ok "EOS"

values.name: decoder
values.comment: Decode a [Symbol](#Symbol) from a JSON value.

values.name: encode
values.comment: Encode a [Symbol](#Symbol) into a JSON value.

values.name: errorToString
values.comment:
You can use this function to convert an [Error](#Error) to a human-readable
`String`.

values.name: fromPrecisionAndCode
values.comment: Create a [Symbol](#Symbol) from a precision and a [SymbolCode](Eos-SymbolCode).

values.name: fromPrecisionAndCodeString
values.comment:
Create a [Symbol](#Symbol) from a precision and a `String` that represents a
[SymbolCode](Eos-SymbolCode). If you already have a [SymbolCode](Eos-SymbolCode),
use [fromPrecisionAndCode](#fromPrecisionAndCode) instead.

    import Eos.SymbolCode

    fromPrecisionAndCodeString 1 "EOS"
        |> Result.map
            (\symbol ->
                { precision = precision symbol
                , codeString = Eos.SymbolCode.toString (code symbol)
                }
            )
    --> Ok { precision = 1, codeString = "EOS" }

    fromPrecisionAndCodeString -1 "EOS"
    --> Err (PrecisionError NegativePrecision)

    fromPrecisionAndCodeString 1 "eos"
    --> Err (SymbolCodeError (Eos.SymbolCode.InvalidCharacters ( 'e', [ 'o', 's' ] )))

values.name: precision
values.comment:
Get the precision of a [Symbol](#Symbol).

    fromPrecisionAndCodeString 1 "EOS"
        |> Result.map precision
    --> Ok 1

name: Eos.SymbolCode

unions.name: Error
unions.comment:
Converting a `String` into a `SymbolCode` (with [fromString](#fromString))
can fail. This type represents all of the possible errors.

    fromString "Test"
    --> Err (InvalidCharacters ( 'e', [ 's', 't' ] ))

    fromString "VERYLONG"
    --> Err TooLong

    fromString "EOS"
        |> Result.map toString
    --> Ok ("EOS")

unions.name: SymbolCode
unions.comment:
The symbol code is the "name" of the symbol. For example, the symbol `0,ABC` has
precision `0` and symbol code `ABC`.

A symbol code is a `String` of up to 7 characters, consisting of uppercase letters
from A-Z.

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
Decode a [SymbolCode](#SymbolCode) from JSON. It already does all of the
validation necessary to ensure the [SymbolCode](#SymbolCode) is valid.

values.name: encode
values.comment: Encode a [SymbolCode](#SymbolCode) into JSON.

values.name: errorToString
values.comment:
You can use this function to convert an [Error](#Error) into a human-readable
`String`.

values.name: fromString
values.comment:
Convert a `String` into a [SymbolCode](#SymbolCode). A SymbolCode must be
a `String` of up to 7 characters, all uppercase letters (A-Z).

    fromString "Test"
    --> Err (InvalidCharacters ( 'e', [ 's', 't' ] ))

    fromString "VERYLONG"
    --> Err TooLong

    fromString "EOS"
        |> Result.map toString
    --> Ok ("EOS")

values.name: toString
values.comment:
Convert a [SymbolCode](#SymbolCode) to a `String`

    fromString "EOS"
        |> Result.map toString
    --> Ok "EOS"

name: Eos.TimePoint

unions.name: TimePoint
unions.comment: A point in time in microseconds.

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
Decode a [TimePoint](#TimePoint) from JSON. You can use this to receive a
[TimePoint](#TimePoint) from the blockchain or some server.

values.name: encode
values.comment:
Encode a [TimePoint](#TimePoint) into JSON. You can use this to send a
[TimePoint](#TimePoint) to the blockchain or some server.

values.name: fromMicroseconds
values.comment: Convert microseconds to a `TimePoint`.

values.name: fromPosix
values.comment:
Convert a `Time.Posix` to a `TimePoint`. Since `Time.Posix` uses milliseconds
and EOSIO uses microseconds, you may lose some information.

values.name: toMicroseconds
values.comment: Convert a `TimePoint` to microseconds.

values.name: toPosix
values.comment:
Convert a `TimePoint` to a `Time.Posix`. Since `Time.Posix` uses milliseconds
and EOSIO uses microseconds, you may lose some information.

name: Eos.TimePointSec

unions.name: TimePointSec
unions.comment: A point in time in seconds.

values.name: decoder
values.comment:
Decode a [TimePointSec](#TimePointSec) from JSON. You can use this to receive a
[TimePointSec](#TimePointSec) from the blockchain or some server.

values.name: encode
values.comment:
Encode a [TimePointSec](#TimePointSec) into JSON. You can use this to send a
[TimePointSec](#TimePointSec) to the blockchain or some server.

values.name: fromPosix
values.comment: Convert a `Time.Posix` to a `TimePointSec`.

values.name: fromSeconds
values.comment: Convert seconds to a `TimePointSec`.

values.name: toPosix
values.comment: Convert a `TimePointSec` to a `Time.Posix`.

values.name: toSeconds
values.comment: Convert a `TimePointSec` to seconds.
